title: Implementing an Email Security Driver
breadcrumbs: Security Drivers and the Security Driver API
text: Implementing an Email Security Driver You can implement a custom email security driver using the Security Driver API. The API include file zsecdrv.h is located in InstallDir /uniface/3gl/include . Compiled libraries for the drivers are provided in the /common/bin (Windows) or /common/lib (Unix) directory of your Uniface installation: zsecdrv —Generic Security Driver uauthlogin —AUTH LOGIN security driver uauthntlm —AUTH NTML security driver uauthsspi —SSPI security driver uauthxoauth2 —XOAUTH2 security drivers for POP and SMTP The C source files for these security drivers are available as a sample on Rocket Community . You can write your own security driver in any language that supports creating a shared library or DLL. To implement your own SMTP driver: Ensure that you understand the way the SMTP driver works, including security context, the use of buffers, and the flow of API calls. For more information, see Security Context , Buffer Management for SMTP Security Drivers and Flow of Calls to the SMTP Security Driver API . Include the definitions provided by the API in your source file. For more information, see APIs: Security Driver . Compile the source into a standard shared library for your platform, where the entry point function is an exported function that may be called. Refer to one of the sample make files provided. Enable the security driver by declaring the entry point of the driver in the [USER_3GL] section of the application assignment file. For example, for Microsoft Windows: [USER_3GL] c:/uniface/bin/mysmtpsecdrv(MySMTPSecDrv) Copy Related Links Support for 3GL Languages Strictly speaking, the interface between Uniface and 3GL is not tied to any specific language. You should be able to use the language of your choice, as long as the certain conditions are met. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
